description: This figure presents a three-panel line graph showing the effect of carbon content (0.3–1.1 wt.%) on the mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel with fixed 9% Al and 30% Mn. The top panel plots ultimate tensile strength (UTS, solid triangles) and 0.2% offset yield strength (0.2% YS, open triangles) in MPa; the middle panel shows ductility via reduction of area (ψ, open circles) and elongation (δ, solid circles) in percent; the bottom panel gives U-notch impact toughness (K_CU, open diamonds) in J/cm². Solid curves denote the aged condition (quenched from 1050°C then aged at 550°C for 16 h), while dashed curves represent the as-quenched state. All strength metrics increase monotonically with carbon, with aged samples showing higher values. Ductility and impact toughness both peak near 0.7–0.8 wt.% C (about 50% ductility and 120 J/cm² K_CU) before declining sharply at higher carbon. The figure supports the conclusion that carbon exceeding 1 wt.% in the aged state causes grain-boundary carbide precipitation, severely degrading toughness, whereas carbon below ~0.7 wt.% promotes δ-ferrite formation.
